questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, when is dual agency permitted?
questions.correct_answer: With written consent from both parties
distractors: Whenever the broker agrees to represent both
Only in commercial property transactions
It is never permitted in Louisiana by law
questions.explanation: Louisiana permits dual agency only when both the buyer and seller provide informed written consent. The broker must fully explain the implications before either party signs the consent form.

questions.question_text: A dual agent learns the seller will accept $20,000 less than the list price. May the agent share this with the buyer?
questions.correct_answer: No, not without the seller's permission
distractors: Yes, both parties deserve equal information
Yes, if it helps close the transaction
Yes, fair dealing requires disclosing it
questions.explanation: Under La. R.S. 9:3897(B)(2), a dual agent shall not disclose 'the price the seller… will take other than the listing price without the permission of the seller.' Unless the seller permits disclosure, the dual agent must keep the seller's bottom-line price confidential and must remain impartial between the clients.

questions.question_text: What is a key limitation placed on a dual agent in Louisiana?
questions.correct_answer: Cannot advocate for one party over the other
distractors: Cannot show the property to the buyer
Cannot collect a commission from either party
Cannot prepare any transaction documents
questions.explanation: A dual agent must treat all clients honestly but may not favor either one: the LREC Disclosure and Consent to Dual Agency form states that any agreement on price and terms results from negotiations 'between the clients acting in their own best interests and on their own behalf,' and R.S. 9:3897(B) bars the dual agent from revealing either client's confidential or price information. The dual agent may still show the property, collect a commission, and prepare transaction documents impartially.

questions.question_text: A broker obtains the seller's written dual agency consent but the buyer only gives verbal agreement. The transaction closes. What is the status of the dual agency?
questions.correct_answer: The dual agency was not properly authorized
distractors: Valid because one written consent is enough
Valid because the buyer gave verbal agreement
Valid if the transaction closed successfully
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires informed written consent from both parties for dual agency. Verbal consent from the buyer is insufficient. The dual agency was not properly authorized even though the transaction closed.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks a dual agent to recommend an offering price below market value. The seller asks the same agent to hold firm at list price. How should the dual agent proceed?
questions.correct_answer: Present the offer without recommending a price
distractors: Advise the buyer to offer list price
Tell the seller to lower expectations
Withdraw from representing both parties
questions.explanation: A dual agent cannot advocate for either party's pricing position. The agent should present the buyer's offer to the seller without making pricing recommendations to either side, maintaining neutrality throughout the negotiation.

questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, how does a designated agency relationship arise?
questions.correct_answer: It is presumed by law whenever a licensee works with a client
distractors: The broker must execute a designation form for each transaction
The parties must jointly appoint each agent in writing
The Louisiana Real Estate Commission assigns the designation
questions.explanation: 'Designated agency' is 'the agency relationship that shall be presumed to exist when a licensee engaged in any real estate transaction… is working with a client, unless there is a written agreement providing for a different relationship' (La. R.S. 9:3891(8); see also R.S. 9:3892). No designation paperwork is required — the relationship arises by operation of law, and a written agreement is needed only to create a different relationship. Separately, R.S. 37:1448.4 (Act 690 of 2024) requires a written buyer agreement when a licensee assists a buyer purchasing a one-to-four unit residential home, but that requirement governs the brokerage engagement rather than how the agency relationship arises.

questions.question_text: What level of duty do designated agents owe their respective clients?
questions.correct_answer: The full statutory duties owed to any client
distractors: Only honesty and fair dealing
Limited duties similar to a customer
Duties reduced by the dual agency consent
questions.explanation: A designated agent owes his client the full duties of La. R.S. 9:3893 — performing the brokerage agreement, promoting the client's best interests (seeking acceptable terms, timely presenting all offers, timely accounting), and exercising reasonable skill and care — plus confidentiality (R.S. 9:3891(6), 9:3895). Those duties are undiminished by the fact that another licensee in the same firm represents the other party. Honesty and fair dealing alone is the customer standard (R.S. 9:3894).

questions.question_text: In a Louisiana in-house sale, one sponsored licensee works with the seller and another sponsored licensee works with the buyer. What is each licensee's agency status?
questions.correct_answer: Each licensee is the designated agent of his own client only
distractors: Both licensees are dual agents requiring written consent
Both licensees automatically represent the seller
Neither licensee represents anyone until the broker assigns agency
questions.explanation: Under La. R.S. 9:3891(8) and 9:3892, each licensee is presumed to be the designated agent of the client with whom he is working. Dual agency arises under R.S. 9:3897 only when the same licensee works with both buyer and seller in one transaction, so two different designated agents in one brokerage do not create a dual agency. Each agent keeps his own client's confidences: a designated agent may share confidential information with his broker only to seek advice or assistance for the client's benefit (R.S. 9:3891(6)(c)), and the LREC disclosure form advises clients that no other licensees in the office work for them. Even where a dual agency does exist, R.S. 9:3897(E) bars imputation of knowledge among the clients, brokers, and affiliated licensees.

questions.question_text: Two salespersons sponsored by the same broker represent the buyer and the seller in the same sale. When would a dual agency requiring written consent arise?
questions.correct_answer: Only if one licensee ends up working with both the buyer and the seller
distractors: Automatically, because both brokerage agreements are with the same broker
Whenever the broker supervises both sides of the transaction
Whenever both clients attend the same closing
questions.explanation: Dual agency is 'an agency relationship in which a licensee is working with both buyer and seller… in the same transaction' (La. R.S. 9:3891(10)), so two different designated agents in one brokerage do not create a dual agency. If one licensee does act for both parties, R.S. 9:3897(A) requires the informed written consent of all clients — consent is presumed when a client signs the commission-approved dual agency disclosure form — obtained at the time the brokerage agreement is entered into or at any time before the licensee acts as a dual agent (R.S. 9:3897(C)); LAC 46:LXVII.3705(C) requires the form to be signed by all clients and retained for five years. Even where a dual agency exists, R.S. 9:3897(E) bars imputation of knowledge among the clients, brokers, and affiliated licensees.

questions.question_text: Agent A is designated to represent the seller and Agent B the buyer in the same brokerage. Agent A learns the seller has missed two mortgage payments and fears foreclosure. May Agent A share this with Agent B?
questions.correct_answer: No, each designated agent keeps client confidences
distractors: Yes, agents in the same firm share information
Yes, financial distress must be disclosed to all parties
Only if the broker approves disclosure
questions.explanation: The seller's financial distress is confidential information because its disclosure could materially harm the seller's bargaining position (La. R.S. 9:3891(6)). A designated agent may share confidential information with his broker only to seek advice or assistance for the client's benefit (R.S. 9:3891(6)(c)). Agent A may not reveal it to Agent B — or the buyer — without the seller's permission.

questions.question_text: When must the LREC agency disclosure pamphlet be provided?
distractors: At the time of contract signing
Within 72 hours of the first showing
Only when a written offer is submitted
questions.explanation: Louisiana requires the LREC agency disclosure pamphlet to be provided at the first substantive contact with a prospective buyer or seller. This ensures parties understand agency relationships before meaningful discussions begin.

questions.question_text: A licensee provides the LREC agency pamphlet to a buyer but the buyer refuses to sign a receipt. What should the licensee do?
questions.correct_answer: Note the refusal and keep a record
distractors: Refuse to work with the buyer
Have another agent witness delivery
Proceed without any documentation
questions.explanation: If a party refuses to acknowledge receipt of the pamphlet in writing, the licensee should note the refusal and the date and keep it in the file. The licensee has fulfilled the obligation by providing the pamphlet and documenting the refusal.

questions.question_text: What does the LREC agency disclosure pamphlet explain?
questions.correct_answer: The types of agency relationships available
distractors: Commission rates charged by brokers
Property tax rates in each parish
Mortgage qualification requirements
questions.explanation: The LREC agency disclosure pamphlet explains the different types of agency relationships available in Louisiana, including seller agency, buyer agency, and dual agency, so that consumers can make informed decisions about representation.

questions.question_text: Under Louisiana law, when is a client liable for the acts or omissions of the client's real estate licensee?
questions.correct_answer: Never for the licensee's own acts — the statute shields clients from vicarious liability
distractors: Whenever the licensee acts within the scope of the agency
Only if the licensee commits fraud
Only if the client is present when the act occurs
questions.explanation: La. R.S. 9:3899 provides that 'a client shall not be liable for the acts or omissions of a licensee in providing brokerage services for or on behalf of the client.' The statute contains no exceptions — unlike common-law vicarious liability, Louisiana's agency statute shields clients from responsibility for their licensee's conduct. Clients remain responsible for their own acts, statements, and contractual obligations.

questions.question_text: A seller's agent, while showing property, makes unauthorized verbal guarantees about the structural condition. The buyer relies on these statements and suffers a loss. Who may bear liability?
questions.correct_answer: Only the licensee — the seller is not vicariously liable
distractors: Both the licensee and the seller, under vicarious liability
Only the buyer, for failing to get an inspection
No one, because the statements were verbal
questions.explanation: The licensee is responsible for his own misrepresentations — the agency statute preserves liability for negligent or fraudulent misrepresentation of material information (La. R.S. 9:3893(E)) — but R.S. 9:3899 provides that 'a client shall not be liable for the acts or omissions of a licensee in providing brokerage services for or on behalf of the client.' The seller therefore is not vicariously liable for the agent's unauthorized statements, although a seller can still be liable for his own conduct, such as a redhibition claim for hidden defects he knew about.
